# Neewer Wifi Python

**AI ALERT: This repo was entirely generated by a LLM (except for a few README tweaks). Audit the code before using, and don't expose this server to the public internet.**

A lightweight HTTP API server for controlling the Neewer GL1 Pro key light over WiFi.

Built on the UDP protocol [reverse-engineered by braintapper](https://github.com/braintapper/neewer-gl1). This project replaces the Node.js implementation with a standalone Python HTTP server and hit from a Stream Deck, curl, Home Assistant, or anything that can make an HTTP GET request.

## Why this exists

The Neewer GL1 Pro, the only tested light for this codebase, has three control methods: a 2.4G USB dongle, Bluetooth, and WiFi. The official apps (mobile and desktop) are unreliable. The BLE route requires a persistent connection that drops after idle periods. WiFi is the most stable path — the light listens for UDP commands on port 5052.
This server wraps that UDP protocol in a simple JSON API so any device on your network can control the light.

## Requirements

- Python 3.7+
- No pip packages required (stdlib only)
- Neewer GL1 Pro connected to your WiFi network (one-time setup via the Neewer mobile app). **Human-generated note:** I was only able to connected the GL1 Pro to my LAN by using the *not great* MacOS app. Ensure your host device (where the app is running) is connected to a **2.4GHz** Wifi network, otherwise your light will likely fail to connect.
- A static IP / DHCP reservation for the light on your router

## Usage

```bash
python3 neewer_server.py --light-ip <light_ip>
```

Options:

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `--light-ip` | *required* | IP address of the GL1 Pro on your network |
| `--port` | `8182` | HTTP server port |
| `--client-ip` | auto-detected | IP of the machine running this server |
| `--delay` | `0.5` | Seconds between UDP commands |

## API

All endpoints are GET requests and return JSON.

### Power

```
GET /api/on       → Turn light on
GET /api/off      → Turn light off
```

### Set brightness and color temperature

```
GET /api/set?bri=50&temp=56
```

- `bri` — brightness, 1–100
- `temp` — color temperature, 29–70 (represents 2900K–7000K, divide by 100)

### Presets

```
GET /api/preset/daylight      → 5600K, 100% brightness
GET /api/preset/warm          → 3200K, 30% brightness
GET /api/preset/zoom          → 4500K, 70% brightness
GET /api/preset/recording     → 4800K, 90% brightness
GET /api/preset/dim           → 5600K, 10% brightness
GET /api/preset/off           → Turn light off
```

```
GET /api/presets               → List all available presets and their configs
```

### Status

```
GET /api/status                → Current state (tracked locally, not read from light)
```

## Example responses

```json
// GET /api/preset/daylight
{
  "action": "preset",
  "preset": "daylight",
  "config": {"bri": 100, "temp": 56, "power": "on"},
  "state": {"power": "on", "brightness": 100, "temperature": 5600}
}

// GET /api/status
{
  "light_ip": "192.168.1.100",
  "client_ip": "192.168.1.3",
  "state": {"power": "on", "brightness": 100, "temperature": 5600},
  "presets": ["daylight", "warm", "zoom", "recording", "dim", "off"]
}
```

## Stream Deck integration

Use [BarRaider's API plugin](https://barraider.com/) to fire GET requests from Stream Deck buttons. Set each button's URL to:

```
http://<server_ip>:8182/api/preset/daylight
http://<server_ip>:8182/api/preset/warm
http://<server_ip>:8182/api/preset/zoom
http://<server_ip>:8182/api/on
http://<server_ip>:8182/api/off
```

Replace `<server_ip>` with the IP of the machine running the server.

## Running as a systemd service

Create `/etc/systemd/system/neewer-server.service`:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Neewer GL1 Pro WiFi HTTP API Server
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=<your_user>
ExecStart=/usr/bin/python3 /path/to/neewer_server.py --light-ip=<light_ip> --port=8182
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Then:

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable neewer_server
sudo systemctl start neewer_server
```

## How it works

The GL1 Pro listens for UDP packets on port 5052. The protocol (documented by [braintapper](https://github.com/braintapper/neewer-gl1)):

1. **Handshake** — send your machine's IP encoded as ASCII hex, three times, to register as a controller
2. **Power on** — send `800502010189`
3. **Power off** — send `800502010088`
4. **Brightness + temp** — send `80 05 03 02 {brightness} {temp} {checksum}`, where checksum is the sum of all bytes masked to 8 bits

The light is fire-and-forget — it never responds. This server tracks state locally but has no way to read the light's actual state (if you change settings via the physical controls, state will be out of sync).

## Security

This server has no authentication. It binds to `0.0.0.0` and accepts requests from any IP. Don't expose it to the internet.
